title: Quantum Advantage over Wirings of Nonsignaling Boxes in Multipartite Networks
authors: Peter Bierhorst, Arkaprabha Ghosal, Soumyadip Patra
abstract: Quantum-entangled measurements are known to enable multi-party behaviors that are impossible with unentangled measurements on nonlocal resources, even those that are super-quantum and bound only by the no-signaling principle. This advantage can be witnessed by the entanglement swapping protocol, along with corresponding impossibility results for "nonlocality swapping". However, the advantage assumes the absence of pre-existing nonlocal resources shared by the swapped-to parties; it no longer holds if all pairs of parties are allowed to share bipartite nonlocal resources. Here, we consider a resource-theoretic perspective in which bipartite nonclassical resources are free resources that can be shared by any pair of parties in a multipartite network, and ask whether quantum entangled measurements can still provide an advantage over certain basic measurements, known as wirings, of nonsignaling nonlocal resources. We resolve this question in the affirmative by demonstrating an explicit four-party behavior that can be achieved with bipartite quantum resources subject to entangled measurements, and cannot be achieved if the bipartite resources are allowed to be more general nonsignaling nonlocal "boxes" so long as the measurements are restricted to local wirings, even also allowing for globally shared classical randomness. Furthermore, the argument generalizes: the same separation can be witnessed for K+2 parties with access to K-partite nonlocal resources for any K > 2. We also examine these results in different contexts, such as the star network configurations and scenarios not admitting globally shared classical randomness, further enhancing understanding of the capabilities of entangled measurements in multi-party configurations.
summary: The paper establishes a robust quantum advantage of entangled measurements over classical/nonsignaling boxes in multi-party settings. By analyzing specific four-party and generalized $K+2$ party protocols, the authors prove that entanglement allows for correlations violating established inequalities, even when local wirings and global randomness are permitted.
main_problem: To determine if quantum entangled measurements provide an advantage over local wirings of nonsignaling nonlocal resources in multipartite networks when bipartite non-classical resources are shared.
main_result: The authors demonstrate a general separation, showing that for $K+2$ parties, quantum entangled measurements can witness behaviors impossible to achieve using only local wirings on nonsignaling boxes, even with global classical randomness.
method: The analysis uses a resource-theoretic approach, comparing the expected values of Bell-type inequalities (like Mao-Bell) achievable via Quantum Entangled Measurements (QEM) versus those constrained by Nonsignaling Boxes (NSW).
model_or_system: The model involves multipartite networks where parties share bipartite nonclassical resources. The comparison is between quantum entanglement/entangled measurements and correlations bounded only by the no-signaling principle.
key_observables: Violation of Bell inequalities, specifically the Mao-Bell inequality ($	ext{BM}$), which quantifies genuine multi-party nonlocality.
important_parameters: The fidelity ($F$) of the shared bipartite quantum states is a key parameter determining the threshold for quantum advantage (e.g., $F > 0.941$).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that the NSW correlations are restricted to local wirings of at-most-$K$-partite nonsignaling resources, and it handles cases with or without globally shared classical randomness.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate network topologies (star vs. fully connected) for $K=2$ parties, and schemes involving GHZ state projections used to induce the testable multi-party behavior.
paper_structure: The paper proceeds by establishing a general framework comparing QEM vs. NSW correlations in multipartite settings. It develops specific protocols (e.g., four-party star network) to witness the advantage, proves the separation for arbitrary $K$, and discusses generalizations regarding classical randomness.
why_interesting: This work rigorously defines the boundary between quantum nonlocality witnessed by entanglement versus mere super-quantum correlations allowed by local no-signaling constraints, which is fundamental to understanding genuine multipartite quantum resources.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Three-party networks of parties Al- ice (A), Bob (B), and Charlie (C) where un- derlying bipartite nonclassical resources are available in (a) a star network with B at the center, and (b) a fully connected configu- ration in which all pairs have access to a bipartite nonclassical resource.

Figure 2: A four-party network in which each of the (six) pairs of parties shares a bipartite nonclassical resource, but there are no higher-order multipartite entangled resources. Globally shared classical randomness, which may be present, is not depicted.
Figure 3: Scheme for generating a behavior in the QEM paradigm, which will not be replicable in the NSW paradigm.

Figure 5: Conditioned on David measuring and receiving outputs from all of his bipartite resources, these resources that were previously shared with other parties as in Figure 4 reduce to unipartite local random variables.
Figure 4: The bipartite resources of Figure 2 can in general be multiple parallel resources measured in cascaded fashion, with outputs of resources fed as inputs to other resources.
